segments.s476-g1: This clause is a temporal subordinate: سکندر (subject) + چو (conjunction meaning 'when') + کرد (past light verb 'did' used with the following noun) + اندر ایران (locative phrase 'into/over Iran') + نگاه (verbal noun 'a look/gaze'). The verb کرد with نگاه forms a light-verb construction equivalent to 'cast a look'. چو here marks time ('when') rather than simile.
segments.s476-g2: بدانست is the main past verb 'he knew/learned'. The following clause 'کو را شد آن تاج و گاه' is an indirect interrogative/relative: کو (here functioning like 'who/which person') marked by را (object/emphatic marker) and شد 'became/fell to', followed by آن تاج و گاه ('that crown and throne/time') — i.e. 'he learned who it was to whom that crown and throne had become'. Note the inverted Persian word order: the person (کو را) is fronted and the thing that 'became' is expressed after شد.
segments.s476-g3: همی is an iterative/continuative adverb here ('kept on/continually'), followed by a coordinated direction phrase راه و بی‌راه ('road and off-road'). لشکر کشید is a light verbal construction meaning 'he moved/marched the army' — کشید is the past of کشیدن used here in the sense 'to draw/lead/bring' rather than the literal agricultural senses in the lemma list.
segments.s476-g4: سوی introduces direction ('toward'). کید هندی is a noun phrase 'Indian stratagem/plot' (کید modified by the following adjective هندی). سپه برکشید is the second verb phrase: سپه 'the host/army' is the object/affected entity and برکشید ('raised/drew up') is the past verb with the prefix بر- giving a sense of 'lift/raise/muster' — overall: 'he directed/raised the host toward the Indian stratagem'.
segments.s476-g5: به جایی که introduces a locative relative clause 'to/at a place that…'. آمد is the past tense 'came/arrived' and سکندر is the subject. فراز is a postpositive adverbial meaning 'up/on high' or 'forth' and here modifies the arrival, a poetic placement typical of Ferdowsi.
segments.s476-g6: در شارستانها sets the location 'in the towns/cities' (شارستانها is the plural). گشادند is a past plural verb 'they opened/were opened' (poetic older form of گشودند/گشایند) and باز is an adverb meaning 'open/wide' here ('opened wide' or 'opened their gates'). The verb agrees in plural with شارستانها.
segments.s476-g7: ازان مرز is a locative/origin phrase 'from that border/region'. کس را (with را as the definite/object/emphatic marker) + به مردم نداشت is an impersonal/experiential structure meaning 'he had no person among the people' or 'there was no one of the populace (for him)'. نداشت is the past negative of داشتن ('did not have'). The clause expresses absence of any local people in that frontier.
segments.s476-g8: ز is the poetic form of 'from'. ناهید here denotes the goddess Anahita. مغفر is an abstract noun 'forgiveness/mercy' and همی is a continuative adverb 'continually/always'. برگذاشت is the past of برگذاشتن 'to leave aside/abandon/withhold' — together: 'he continually left aside/withheld Anahita's forgiveness' (i.e. he did not receive or grant that mercy).
segments.s476-g33: وگر is the conditional particle 'if'/'but if' introducing the protasis. بگذری is 2nd-person singular present subjunctive (classical Persian uses this form for a conditional: 'if you pass/ignore'). زین is the contracted prepositional phrase زِ + این = 'from/of this' (poetic form), and سخن means 'speech/warning'. نگذرم is 1st-person present negative (ن + گذرم) meaning 'I will not let it pass' — idiomatically 'I will not forgive/overlook' and serves as the apodosis of the condition.
segments.s476-g34: سر و تاج و تختت lists three objects (head, crown, and your throne); the possessive suffix -ت on تختت is attached only to the last noun but semantically applies to the whole list. به پی is an archaic/prepositional phrase meaning 'to (those) in pursuit' or 'into the hands of pursuers'. بسپرم is 1st-person present (subjunctive/anticipatory) of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over' — the verb completes the clause with the objects placed before it in typical Persian order.
segments.s476-g17: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'. The phrase places the proper name اسکندر followed by two epithets راد (generous) and پیروزگر (conqueror) after it — adjectives and epithets are postpositive in Persian poetry. There is no finite verb inside this segment; it functions as the source/agent for the following nominal comment.
segments.s476-g18: خداوند here means 'lord/sovereign' (an honorific/title) rather than the monotheistic God; in poetic usage it names the possessor or master. The coordinated nouns شمشیر و تاج و کمر ('sword, crown, and girdle') follow without an ezafe — a common poetic omission — and are the regalia/possessions associated with that lord. The whole segment supplies the thing(s) that come from the named source.
segments.s476-g19: سر نامه is a compound meaning 'the head/opening of the book' (سر = top/opening + نامه = book). بود is the past copula 'was', and آفرین here is a noun 'praise/acclamation' not the interjection. از نخست is an adverbial phrase 'from the beginning/at first' modifying the statement.
segments.s476-g20: بدانکس is a dative phrase 'to/for that person'; که introduces a relative clause 'who...'. دل را is a marked definite object ('the heart' with the object marker را). به دانش is an instrument/means phrase 'by/with knowledge', and بشست is the past tense 'he washed' (an older/poetic past form with the b- prefix). The clause means 'to that man who washed his heart with knowledge'.
segments.s476-g21: ز کار means 'from/work among tasks'; آن is a demonstrative 'that'; گزیند is a present (poetic) third-person form 'he chooses' (گزیدن). The following relative کــه بی‌رنج‌تر modifies آن: 'that which is less toilful/less painful' — بی‌رنج‌تر is a comparative adjective 'more free of toil' (i.e. less arduous).
segments.s476-g22: چو here is temporal 'when'; خواهد is the present 'he wishes/wants'. که introduces the subordinate clause; بردارد is a present/subjunctive-type form of برداشتن 'take up/remove' (following خواهد it expresses 'that he take' / 'he may take' or simply 'he takes' in narrative). از گنج is the source 'from the treasure', and بر is a short directional/poetic particle ('up/to/away') finishing the phrasal sense 'take up from'.
segments.s476-g23: گراینده is a participial/agent noun 'one who turns/turns toward' or 'the one who is guided'. باشد is the subjunctive/optative copula 'may be / is' (classical Persian uses باشد for a neutral 'be'). به یزدان پاک is the prepositional phrase 'to/unto the Pure God' (یزدان پاک = 'the Pure God'). The clause states the condition of turning toward God.
segments.s476-g24: بدو is a dative 'to him' (i.e. the person who turns to God). دارد is the present 'has'; امید is the object 'hope' ('has hope toward him'). The coordinated phrase و زو ترس و باک uses زو (poetic contraction of از او, 'from him') to mark the source of ترس و باک 'fear and dread' — so the balance is that toward God one should have both hope and fear. Note the older/poetic pronoun contraction and the repetition of and (و) to list feelings.
segments.s476-g9: چو + آمد is a temporal clause meaning “when he came” (چو = when). بران is an older contracted prepositional phrase (بر آن) meaning “to/onto that,” followed by the place noun شارستان بزرگ (“great city”). Word order is temporal marker + verb + locative phrase.
segments.s476-g10: که here introduces a subordinate/relative clause (“where/which”). میلاد functions as a proper name (subject) and خواندیش is an archaic/periphrastic past form (خواند + ـیش) meaning “he read it” or “he read there.” کید سترگ is the object phrase “a great/mighty stratagem,” with کید = plot/stratagem and سترگ used poetically as an intensifier; the noun + modifier order is normal in older Persian poetry.
segments.s476-g11: بران (بر آن) again marks the location/target (“to that”), مرز = “border/frontier” names the place, لشکر is the direct object “army/host,” and فرود آورید is a poetic/archaic past plural form meaning “they brought down” or “they alighted/placed” (compare modern آوردند). The clause means they deployed an army at that border.
segments.s476-g12: همه quantifies the following noun phrase (همه بوم ایشان = “all their land/realm”). سپه گسترید is a verbal phrase in which سپه (“host/army”) is combined with گسترید (past plural of گستراندن, “they spread/extended”), so the whole means “they spread the host over all their land.” Note the verb placed after its object phrase in this poetic order.
segments.s476-g13: نویسنده نامه is a genitive/attributive group “the writer of the letter.” را is the definite object marker. خواندند here means “they called/summoned” (a classical use of خواندن) rather than just “they read,” so the clause says they summoned the scribe.
segments.s476-g14: به پیش = “into the presence of / before.” سکندرش is the name سکندر (Iskandar/Alexander) in an oblique/conjoined form (the final ـش is an enclitic/pronoun shape in classical orthography); بنشاندند is past plural “they seated/placed (him).” Together: “they seated him before Iskandar.”
segments.s476-g15: یکی = “a certain one / someone.” نامه بنوشت is an archaic past formulation meaning “wrote a letter” (بنوشت is an older/past form of نوشتن). نزدیک کید can be read adverbially as “near the time/of the plot” or “the plot drew near”; syntactically the line is compact/elliptical, so نزدیک modifies the event of the stratagem (کید = “plot/stratagem”).
segments.s476-g16: چو = “as/like” introduces a simile. شیری here means “a lion-like (creature)” or simply “a lion.” که begins a relative clause: ارغنده گردد به صید = “becomes aroused/turns (when) to the hunt.” گردد is a present/past-general verbal form meaning “becomes/turns” and به صید = “to the hunt/for prey.” The whole simile: like a lion roused when it goes after prey.
segments.s476-g25: بداند is a third-person present/subjunctive form of دانستن used here in a jussive/optative sense ('let him know' / 'he should know'). که introduces the subordinate content clause. تخت را places 'throne' before the predicate and is marked by the definite object/topic marker را; مایه‌ایم is a noun (مایه) plus the present copula -ایم ('we are'), so the clause literally reads 'know that we are the mây(e)/cause/support of the throne.'
segments.s476-g26: This clause mirrors the previous structure: جهاندار پیروز forms a noun phrase 'the victorious sovereign' (adjective after noun), را again marks it as definite/object/topic, and سایه‌ایم is سایه + -ایم 'we are the shadow/protection.' The order (noun phrase + را + predicate) is a poetic inversion but grammatical in Persian.
segments.s476-g27: نوشتم is the simple past 'I wrote.' یکی نامه is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/one letter.' نزدیک تو here places a locative/recipient expression after the noun—literally 'near you' but functioning idiomatically as 'for/ addressed to you'; word order (verb — modifier — noun — locative) is common in classical Persian.
segments.s476-g28: که introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that' / 'that'). روشن کند uses روشن (adjectival/noun 'light/enlightening') plus کند (third-person present/subjunctive or jussive of کردن) to mean 'may make/enlighten.' جان تاریک تو is the object phrase 'your dark soul' with the possessor تو following the noun in typical Persian order.
segments.s476-g29: هم‌آنگه که begins a temporal clause 'just then/at that very time that.' بر تو is the prepositional phrase 'to/unto you' (بر + pronoun), and بخواند is the third-person present/subjunctive/optative of خواندن ('he may read' / 'reads'), with دبیر ('the scribe/secretary') as the agent; word order here places the verb before its explicit subject, which is common in poetry.
segments.s476-g30: منه پیش combines منه ('from him/it') + پیش ('before / in the presence of') and yields a phrase meaning 'do not (take) from him in front (of others)'; the clause is completed by the negative imperative مگیر ('do not take'). و links the two commands, and این را is the object 'this' (marked by را) before سگالش, an uncommon/rare noun; سگالش مگیر is another negative imperative 'do not take/seize (the) segâlesh.'
segments.s476-g31: اگر ... رسد is a standard conditional 'if ... comes/arrives' (رسد is present/subjunctive form of رسیدن). روشنی را is fronted and marked with را ('the light' as definite object/topic). مپای is an archaic/rare verbal form (here used prohibitorily) meaning roughly 'do not follow/seek/attach to' and functions as the negative imperative counterpart to the conditional clause.
segments.s476-g32: هم‌اندر زمان is an adverbial phrase 'at the same time / together with the time.' سوی is the preposition 'toward,' and فرمان گرای is a compound where فرمان 'command' is followed by گرای (an element meaning 'inclination' or a '-oriented' notion); together they mean 'toward the command's inclination' or 'turn toward the command/decree.' The whole clause gives a simultaneous instruction complementary to the prior negative.

sentences.s476-s1: When Alexander cast his gaze over Iran, he learned to whom that crown and throne had fallen.
sentences.s476-s2: He marched his army by road and off-road, and turned the host toward the Indian stratagem.
sentences.s476-s3: When Alexander arrived at that place, the towns opened wide.
sentences.s476-s4: From that border he had no one among the people; he constantly withheld Anahita's forgiveness.
sentences.s476-s17: But if you pass over this speech (ignore this warning), I will not forgive; I will hand over your head, crown and throne to those who pursue.
sentences.s476-s9: From Alexander, the generous conqueror, came the lord of the sword, the crown, and the girdle.
sentences.s476-s10: The opening of the book was praise from the first — for that man who washed his heart with knowledge.
sentences.s476-s11: From among tasks he chooses that which is less distressing; when he wishes, he takes from the treasure.
sentences.s476-s12: If one turns to the Pure God, he has hope in Him, and from Him fear and dread.
sentences.s476-s5: When he came to that great city, Milad read there of a mighty stratagem.
sentences.s476-s6: To that border they brought down an army; across all their lands they spread the host.
sentences.s476-s7: They called (summoned) the letter’s writer and set him down before Iskandar.
sentences.s476-s8: A certain man wrote a letter; the stratagem drew near — like a lion that rouses itself when going after prey.
sentences.s476-s13: Let him know that we are the support of the throne; we are the shade (protection) of the victorious sovereign.
sentences.s476-s14: I wrote a certain letter for you, so that it might enlighten your dark soul.
sentences.s476-s15: Just at the moment the scribe reads it to you, do not take it from him in public, and do not seize this 'sagâlesh'.
sentences.s476-s16: If night comes, do not pursue the light; at the same time, steer/turn (things) toward the command's inclination.
